content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

September 21, 1976


Mr. Walter I. Colby
County Counsel
County of Yuba
Courthouse
Marysville, California 95901

Dear Mr. Colby:

This letter is in response to your letter to the Commission of September 3, 1976, requesting advice pursuant to
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329. It also is intended to acknowledge the
substance of a telephone conversation you and I had on September
9, 1976.

The facts, as we understand them, are as follows: A member of the Board of Supervisors owns land which will be directly
affected by a proposed change in zoning of portions of the County.
This change will, in essence, rezone certain agricultural land to
permit limited residential development. It is anticipated that this
change will materially increase the value of the land. The Supervisor in question is one of approximately 20 persons holding interests
in the affected area and he does not use the land as a residence.

We think it is clear, and you apparently agree, that the
Supervisor has a financial interest in the decision, as defined in
Government Code Section 87103, and that he, therefore, should not
"make, participate in the making, or use his official position to
influence the decision." Government Code Section 87100. See,
Opinion requested by Thomas W. Oglesby, 1 FPPC Opinions 71 (No.
75-083, July 2, 1975). In other words, the Supervisor should disqualify himself in the matter.

There remains, however, the question of what the Supervisor may do in order to protect his own interests in this matter.
We note that the Commission's regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section
1870(d) and (f), is apposite. The regulation provides that "making or participating in the making of a governmental decision" and
"in any way attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental decision" shall not include:

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-6001 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-[illegible]

Mr. Walter I. Colby '
Page Two
September 21, 1976

Appearances by a public official as a member of the
general public before an agency in the course of its
prescribed governmental function to represent himself
or herself on matters related solely to his or her personal interests ...

2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700 (d) and (f)

This exception was designed to insure a proper balance between seemingly competing interests of insuring unbiased official decision making and of protecting the rights of public officials whose interests are being affected by governmental decisions. Pursuant to the exception, it is permissible for the Supervisor in question to disqualify himself from voting or participating in the official discussions of the matter in question and to refrain from using his official position to influence those discussions; to "step-down" into the audience and to appear as a member of the public on his own behalf. Of course, in so doing the appearance by the Supervisor must be constrained by the same procedures (time limits, etc.) that would condition the appearance of other members of the public. Further more, the regulation contemplates appearances by the public official related solely to his personal interests. The exception would not apply to any appearance by the official before his or her agency in a representative capacity.

I also want to mention that the Commission is going to discuss the meaning of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Sections 18700 (d) and (f) during its October meeting. If the Commission's conclusions are different than those expressed in this letter, I will contact you. However, pending any determination by the Commission that the advice herein is incorrect, the Supervisor is entitled to rely on the advice and will not be subject to any enforcement proceeding by the Fair Political Practices Commission as a result of such reliance. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18329 (e).

I trust this advice resolves your question. If we can be of further assistance, please do not hesitate to call me.

Sincerely,

Kenneth W. Goshorn
Research Specialist

KWG:jo

State of California

Memorandum

To      : Ken Finney and participants in
          Opinion Request Meeting

Date    : September 14, 197[7]


From    : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
          Ken Goshorn

Subject: Walter Colby letter of September 3.


On September 9, 1976, I spoke with Mr. Colby on the phone - the
following additional facts were developed.

1.  The zoning change in question is to rezone agricultural
    land on the outskirts of the City of Marysville so that
    5-acre residential lots may be developed.

2.  It is foreseeable that this change will be reflected
    in a material increase in value to the land in question.

3.  The land owned by the concerned official is within the
    area and is not just a residence.

4.  Only about 20 land owners will be directly affected by
    the zoning change.  They own varying amounts of property.
    The official is one of these 20 or so people.

5.  Mr. Colby also wants answered the question of what the
    official may do under 18700(f) of the Regulations.


KWG:jhm

OFFICE OF

WALTER I. COLBY

THE COUNTY COUNSEL

COUNTY COUNSEL

COURTHOUSE
MARYSVILLE, CALIF. 95901
(916) 743-1511 Ext. 277

JAMES A. REICHLE
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL

County of Yuba

September 3, 1976

Fair Political Practice Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, Ca. 95814

Re: Request for Opinion

Gentlemen:

Pursuant to Regulations Section 18329, this office requests advice on the following issue:

May a member of the Board of Supervisors vote or participate in the deliberations of the Board in a matter involving change of zoning of a portion of a county in which he owns land which would be affected by such change of zoning?

Very truly yours,

WALTER I. COLBY
County Counsel

WIC:mm
cc: Jim Martin, Chairman
Board of Supervisors
cc: Neal Denny

sections.question: What may a Board of Supervisors member do to protect his own financial interests when he owns land that will be directly affected by a proposed zoning change, given that he must disqualify himself from the official decision?
sections.conclusion: It is permissible for the Supervisor in question to disqualify himself from voting or participating in the official discussions of the matter in question and to refrain from using his official position to influence those discussions; to 'step-down' into the audience; and to appear as a member of the public on his own behalf. Such appearance must be constrained by the same procedures (time limits, etc.) that would condition the appearance of other members of the public and must relate solely to his personal interests, not in any representative capacity.
sections.question_synthetic: A county supervisor owns land that will be materially affected by a proposed zoning change and must disqualify himself from voting on the decision. The question is what steps the supervisor may take to represent and protect his personal financial interests in the matter.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The supervisor may disqualify himself from the official decision-making process and then appear before the agency as a private citizen to represent his own personal financial interests, subject to the same procedural constraints as any other member of the public. This exception balances the need for unbiased official decision-making with the protection of public officials' rights when their interests are affected.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes an FPPC internal memorandum dated September 14, 1976 summarizing facts developed during a phone call, and an incoming request letter from County Counsel Walter Colby dated September 3, 1976. The main advice letter is dated September 21, 1976 and signed by Kenneth W. Goshorn. Clear Q/C structure present with explicit conclusion. Includes caveat that FPPC will discuss the regulation during October meeting and will contact if conclusions change.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What may a Board of Supervisors member do to protect his own financial interests when he owns land that will be directly affected by a proposed zoning change, given that he must disqualify himself from the official decision?

CONCLUSION: It is permissible for the Supervisor in question to disqualify himself from voting or participating in the official discussions of the matter in question and to refrain from using his official position to influence those discussions; to 'step-down' into the audience; and to appear as a member of the public on his own behalf. Such appearance must be constrained by the same procedures (time limits, etc.) that would condition the appearance of other members of the public and must relate solely to his personal interests, not in any representative capacity.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that a County Supervisor with a financial interest in a zoning decision must disqualify himself from voting or participating in official discussions, but may then appear before the agency as a private citizen to represent his personal interests. The supervisor is entitled to rely on this advice and will not be subject to enforcement proceedings by the FPPC.
